annotations.label: Inverse trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: are the inverse functions of the trigonometric functions , under suitably restricted domains

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Real.arcsin, Real.arccos and Real.arctan as inverses of sin/cos/tan on the standard restricted domains.

annotations.label: Arc-prefix notation

anchor.snippet: The most common convention is to name inverse trigonometric functions using an arc- prefix

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the arc- prefix convention but the notational convention itself is not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Herschel superscript notation

anchor.snippet: as introduced by John Herschel in 1813

annotations.note: Historical notational convention; nothing to formalize in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Principal value

anchor.snippet: the function may be restricted to its principal branch

annotations.note: Mathlib's arcsin/arccos/arctan are defined on their principal branches (e.g. range_arctan = Ioo (-(π/2)) (π/2)) but 'principal value' as a general concept is not separately introduced.

annotations.label: Alternative range of arcsecant

anchor.snippet: Some authors define the range of arcsecant to be

annotations.note: Mathlib has no arcsec/arccsc definitions, so alternative ranges of arcsec are not in scope.

annotations.label: Domain of cotangent and cosecant

anchor.snippet: Domain of cotangent

annotations.note: Mathlib has no arccot/arccsc definitions and no explicit principal-domain statement for cot/csc.

annotations.label: Domain of tangent and secant

anchor.snippet: Domain of tangent

annotations.note: Mathlib has no arcsec definition and no explicit principal-domain construct combining sec and tan.

annotations.label: Periodicity of trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: Each of the trigonometric functions is periodic in the real part of its argument

annotations.note: Real.sin_periodic, Real.cos_periodic, Real.tan_periodic are all stated as Function.Periodic instances.

annotations.label: General solutions via inverse trig functions

anchor.snippet: The following table shows how inverse trigonometric functions may be used to solve equalities

annotations.note: Mathlib has arcsin_sin/arccos_cos/arctan_tan inversion lemmas on restricted domains, but does not state the general-solution-set parameterization.

annotations.label: Cosine equation solution without plus-or-minus

anchor.snippet: With the help of integer parity

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma encoding the integer-parity reformulation of the cos x = a solution set.

anchors.snippet: With the help of integer parity

annotations.label: Plus-or-minus symbol clarification

anchor.section: Detailed example and explanation of the "plus or minus" symbol ±
anchor.snippet: whose meaning is now clarified

annotations.note: Expository clarification, not a formalizable statement.

anchors.section: Detailed example and explanation of the "plus or minus" symbol ±

annotations.label: Equal trigonometric values relation

anchor.snippet: The table below shows how two angles

annotations.note: Some sin_eq_sin_iff style lemmas exist for periodic identification, but the full table of equality conditions is not assembled.

annotations.label: Transforming equations via reflection and shift

anchor.snippet: The equations above can be transformed by using the reflection and shift identities

annotations.note: Reflection and shift identities like sin_neg, sin_add_pi, cos_pi_sub exist in Mathlib but the meta-transformation of equation solutions is not stated.

annotations.label: Trigonometric functions of inverse trig functions

anchor.section: Relationships between trigonometric functions and inverse trigonometric functions
anchor.snippet: Trigonometric functions of inverse trigonometric functions are tabulated below

annotations.note: Mathlib has sin_arcsin, cos_arcsin, sin_arccos, cos_arccos, tan_arctan, sin_arctan, cos_arctan giving the standard composition formulas.

annotations.label: Complementary angles identity

anchor.section: Relationships among the inverse trigonometric functions
anchor.snippet: Complementary angles

annotations.note: Real.arccos_eq_pi_div_two_sub_arcsin states arccos x = π/2 - arcsin x exactly.

annotations.label: Negative arguments identity

anchor.section: Relationships among the inverse trigonometric functions
anchor.snippet: Negative arguments

annotations.note: Real.arcsin_neg, Real.arccos_neg, and Real.arctan_neg give the odd/reflection identities for negative inputs.

annotations.label: Reciprocal arguments identity

anchor.section: Relationships among the inverse trigonometric functions
anchor.snippet: Reciprocal arguments

annotations.note: Real.arctan_inv_of_pos and arctan_inv_of_neg formalize the arctan reciprocal identity; arcsec/arccsc reciprocal forms are absent.

annotations.label: Half-angle identity for inverse trig

anchor.section: Relationships among the inverse trigonometric functions
anchor.snippet: From the half-angle formula

annotations.note: No explicit Mathlib lemma deriving inverse-trig half-angle identities like arctan x = 2·arctan(x/(1+√(1+x²))).

annotations.label: Arctangent addition formula

anchor.snippet: This is derived from the tangent addition formula

annotations.note: Real.arctan_add with the x*y<1 hypothesis, plus arctan_add_eq_add_pi/sub_pi for the other cases.

annotations.label: Derivatives of inverse trig functions

anchor.snippet: The derivatives for complex values of z are as follows

annotations.note: Real.hasDerivAt_arctan, Real.deriv_arcsin (in InverseDeriv) and related lemmas formalize the standard derivative formulas; complex versions for arctan exist but arcsin/arccos derivatives are real-only.

annotations.label: Definite integral expression

anchor.snippet: Integrating the derivative and fixing the value at one point gives an expression for the inverse trigonometric function as a definite integral

annotations.note: Mathlib evaluates ∫ 1/(1+x²) dx via Real.deriv_arctan and has improper integrals of 1/(1+x²) over Ioi/Iic, but does not package the result as 'arctan x = ∫₀ˣ dt/(1+t²)'.

annotations.label: Power series for arcsine

anchor.snippet: For arcsine, the series can be derived by expanding its derivative

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma states the arcsin Taylor series ∑ (2n choose n)/(4ⁿ(2n+1)) · x^(2n+1).

annotations.label: Gregory's series for arctangent

anchor.snippet: The Taylor series for arctangent is also known as the arctangent series or Gregory's series

annotations.note: Real.hasSum_arctan and Complex.hasSum_arctan give the Gregory/Leibniz power series for arctan on the open unit disk.

anchors.snippet: The Taylor series for arctangent is also known as the arctangent series or Gregory's series

annotations.label: Euler's series for arctangent

anchor.snippet: Leonhard Euler found a series for the arctangent that converges more quickly than its Taylor series

annotations.note: Euler's accelerated arctangent series is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continued fractions for arctangent

anchor.snippet: Two alternatives to the power series for arctangent are these generalized continued fractions

annotations.note: No generalized continued fraction expansion of arctan is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Indefinite integrals of inverse trig functions

anchor.section: Indefinite integrals of inverse trigonometric functions
anchor.snippet: For real and complex values of z

annotations.note: No general antiderivative formulas like ∫ arcsin z dz = z·arcsin z + √(1-z²) are in Mathlib (only a special definite integral appears in an Archive file).

annotations.label: Integration by parts for arcsine

anchor.snippet: i.e. integration by parts

annotations.note: Worked example, not a separate theorem; not formalized.

annotations.label: Extension to the complex plane

anchor.snippet: One possible way of defining the extension is

annotations.note: Complex.arctan is defined in Mathlib, but Complex.arcsin and Complex.arccos are not.

annotations.label: Logarithmic forms of inverse trig functions

anchor.snippet: These functions may also be expressed using complex logarithms

annotations.note: Complex.arctan is defined directly as -I/2 · log((1+zI)/(1-zI)); logarithmic forms for arcsin/arccos are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalization via Euler's formula

anchor.snippet: they can be generalized by using Euler's formula to form a right triangle in the complex plane

annotations.note: The generalized 'Euler-formula right triangle' construction for inverse trig in the complex plane is not formalized.

annotations.label: Example proof of logarithmic form

anchor.snippet: Using the exponential definition of sine

annotations.note: Expository derivation, not a separate theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finding the angle of a right triangle

anchor.section: Finding the angle of a right triangle
anchor.snippet: suppose a roof drops 8 feet as it runs out 20 feet

annotations.note: Numerical example; not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Two-argument arctangent (atan2)

anchor.snippet: The two-argument atan2 function computes the arctangent of y / x given y and x

annotations.note: Mathlib has Complex.arg, which plays the role of atan2 on the complex plane, but no standalone Real atan2 function.

annotations.label: Arctangent with location parameter

anchor.snippet: The adequate solution is produced by the parameter modified arctangent function

annotations.note: No location-parameter arctan variant in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Numerical accuracy near singular angles

anchor.snippet: For angles near 0 and π , arccosine is ill-conditioned

annotations.note: Numerical conditioning analysis is outside Mathlib's symbolic scope.

annotations.label: Trigonometric functions are not one-to-one

anchor.snippet: Since none of the six trigonometric functions are one-to-one

annotations.note: Non-injectivity of the six trig functions follows from their periodicity in Mathlib, but a packaged 'not Injective' statement for each is not a single named lemma.

annotations.label: atan2 as principal argument of complex number

anchor.snippet: It also equals the principal value of the argument of the complex number x + iy

annotations.note: Complex.arg returns the principal argument in (-π, π], matching the atan2(y, x) interpretation as the argument of x + iy.
